body.title: [sprint] Implement /critic skill — adversarial analysis on targets
body.intention: I intend that /critic provides a callable, testable interface for adversarial reasoning on any codebase artifact.
body.description:
/critic <target> skill for testing and refining adversarial reasoning. Input: target (symbol name, module path, ticket ID, or free-text). Output: structured critique. Implementation: (1) Parse input to identify target type (symbol → search codebase, module → read and parse, ticket → read from queue, free-text → use as-is); (2) Fetch relevant context (code, docs, prior decisions, related tickets); (3) Call Claude (or Igor cognition if available) with prompt: "You are a critic. Analyze [target]. What assumptions are questionable? What could silently break? What's incomplete? What would a skeptic challenge?" (4) Structure output: JSON with sections {assumptions, gaps, risks, suggestions, confidence_level}. (5) Cache results for 1 hour (same target, same context, return cached unless user specifies --refresh).

**Affected files:** skills/critic/ (new directory), skills/critic/cli.py (skill entry point)
**Design rules:** none apply (experimental skill, not yet in critical path)
**Scope boundary:** Skill implementation only; not integrated into build/dispatch; testing is separate ticket
**Completion criteria:** (1) Skill callable via /critic <target>; (2) accepts symbol/module/ticket/text input; (3) fetches context correctly; (4) LLM prompt produces useful critique; (5) output is JSON; (6) caching works; (7) --refresh flag works
**Test plan:** (1) Test with a symbol (function name), verify context fetched; (2) Test with a module path, verify code read; (3) Test with ticket ID, verify description read; (4) Test with free-text, verify prompt used as-is; (5) Run twice, verify cache hit on second; (6) --refresh flag, verify fresh call; (7) Spot-check output: is critique useful? Does it find real issues?
